factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies market using ANZSIC C2221 or ANZSIC06 C2221 classification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly identifies the industry as “ANZSIC code C2221NZ” in the Market Revenue section, which corresponds to the ANZSIC C2221 structural steel fabricating classification.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States employment figures in range 4,200-4,600 for multiple years within 2018-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives employment figures such as 4,100 (2025) and 4,011 (2026), but it does not state employment figures in the 4,200–4,600 range for multiple years between 2018 and 2024. It also does not provide a multi-year 2018–2024 employment series within that range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States market revenue approximately NZD 1.4-1.9 billion for 2023-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not state a 2023–2024 market revenue range of approximately NZD 1.4–1.9 billion. It instead cites a market value of about NZD 1.8 billion for 2025–2026 and provides other IBISWorld figures, but no explicit 2023–2024 revenue estimate in the required range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States domestic fabrication capacity between 120,000-150,000 tonnes per annum
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that total fabrication capacity “expanded from 120,000 to 150,000 tonnes” and later lists “2018–2020: 120,000 tonnes per annum” and “September 2023: 150,000 tonnes per annum,” which is within the 120,000–150,000 tpa range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides geographic locations for at least 3 major fabricators (e.g., Auckland, Christchurch, Whangārei, Lower Hutt, Timaru)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides geographic locations for at least three major fabricators, including Culham Engineering (Whangārei, Auckland, Kawerau, Tokoroa), D&H Steel Construction (Henderson, West Auckland), and Pacific Steel (Auckland facility). These locations are explicitly stated in the fabricator profiles.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies HS codes 7207-7216 or relevant subset for structural steel imports
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly lists HS codes 7207 through 7216 in a table under the “NZ Import Tariff Rates for Raw Steel (HS 7207–7216)” section, covering the requested structural steel import code range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States most structural steel inputs under HS 7207-7216 are duty-free under NZ Normal Tariff, while noting some tariff lines (e.g., certain 7216 sections) carry 5% rates
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists HS 7207–7216 with a “Standard MFN Rate” of 5% and notes preferential 0% rates under FTAs, but it does not state that most structural steel inputs are duty-free specifically under the NZ Normal Tariff. It also does not single out that only some tariff lines (e.g., certain 7216 sections) carry 5% while most are duty-free; instead it applies 5% broadly across all listed codes.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link steel demand: approximately 30,000-32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000-5,000 tonnes structural
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states City Rail Link steel demand in the summary table as “4,000t structural; 31,613t reinforcing,” which includes reinforcing tonnage within the required ~30,000–32,000 range. It does not state reinforcing as “approximately 30,000–32,000 tonnes” nor structural as “3,000–5,000 tonnes,” but the figures given satisfy the criterion’s approximate ranges.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link completion or opening circa 2026
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the City Rail Link is “on track to open in the second half of 2026” and reiterates “CRL opening H2 2026,” which satisfies completion/opening circa 2026.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium roof steel approximately 9,000 tonnes
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not mention Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium at all, and it provides no figure for the roof steel tonnage. Therefore it fails to state that the Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium roof steel is approximately 9,000 tonnes.

The response discusses multiple infrastructure projects and timelines (e.g., City Rail Link opening H2 2026) but does not mention Te Kaha Stadium at all, nor does it state an opening year for it. Therefore, it fails to state that Te Kaha Stadium opens in 2026.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies New Dunedin Hospital as major infrastructure project requiring significant structural steel components
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes “New Dunedin Hospital (Project Whakatuputupu)” as one of the three major infrastructure projects and provides detailed structural steel requirements (e.g., ~1,600 fabricated steel units, beam sizes/weights, and steel erection timing), clearly identifying it as a major steel-intensive project.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Reconciles conflicting market size figures by explaining scope definition differences (narrow product vs. broad service boundaries)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly reconciles conflicting figures by attributing differences to scope definitions, e.g., “pure fabrication enterprises” (ANZSIC C2221NZ/IBISWorld) versus broader steel industry counts (SCNZ/BERL including erectors/distributors), and similarly distinguishes “pure fabricators” from distributors with fabrication capability.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes construction fabricators from distributors/mills/producers when identifying top companies (e.g., Steel & Tube, Pacific Steel)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly distinguishes “pure structural steel fabricators” from “steel distributors and processors that also fabricate,” and it labels Steel & Tube and Fletcher Steel as distributor/fabricator entities rather than pure fabricators.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains why low/zero tariffs remove price umbrella, making margins dependent on global mill prices/FX/freight rather than border protection
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response notes that most steel imports enter New Zealand duty-free under FTAs and that the MFN 5% applies only to non-FTA sources, but it does not explain the “price umbrella” mechanism. It also does not connect low/zero tariffs to margins being driven by global mill prices, FX movements, and freight costs rather than border protection.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies actionable method to obtain class-level data (e.g., filter Stats NZ AES CSV by ANZSIC L4 code for C2221)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not provide an actionable method for obtaining class-level data from Stats NZ or MBIE (e.g., downloading AES CSV and filtering by ANZSIC L4 code C2221). It links to Figure.NZ and cites IBISWorld/SCNZ, but gives no step-by-step instructions for extracting class-level figures from Stats NZ datasets.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects employment or revenue trends to construction cycle timing (e.g., 2023 peak preceding mid-2022 slowdown)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response links industry capacity/throughput and spare capacity changes to the construction cycle, describing a post-COVID downturn and reduced construction activity (e.g., construction activity falling from 2023 to 2025) and noting spare capacity peaking around September 2023 then shrinking by March 2024 as recovery begins.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Validates stated fabrication capacity/throughput by aggregating steel demand across multiple named projects with tonnages
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response lists several projects and includes some steel quantities (e.g., CRL 4,000t structural/31,613t reinforcing; Te Ara Pekapeka Bridge 2,650t), but it does not aggregate steel demand across multiple named projects to validate any stated fabrication capacity/throughput. There is no explicit summation comparing total project tonnage to industry capacity or throughput figures.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies absent official tonnage series and proposes specific triangulation approach using SCNZ capacity, trade data, or project disclosures
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not identify that official Stats NZ/MBIE tonnage-fabricated series are absent, nor does it propose a concrete triangulation method to estimate tonnage using SCNZ capacity, trade (HS) import/export volumes, or project-level steel disclosures. It asserts tonnage figures (e.g., ~100,000 t/yr) but does not lay out a specific triangulation approach to derive them.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Attributes fabricator margin pressure or competitive challenges to NZ import tariffs or border protection rather than global price/FX exposure
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly attributes margin pressure and competitive challenges to global factors like US Section 232 tariffs, global steel overcapacity/diversion, and FX/pricing dynamics, not to NZ import tariffs or border protection. It notes NZ tariffs are mostly 0% under FTAs and only a modest 5% MFN in limited cases, so it does not blame NZ border protection for margin pressure.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents all quantitative data with explicit units (NZD, tonnes, %), specific timeframes (years/FY), and measurement precision
presentation-quality.explanation: Many quantitative figures include units (e.g., NZD, tonnes, %, km, m³) and some timeframes (e.g., FY25, 1H26, March 2026 quarter). However, several key numbers are missing explicit units or precision (e.g., “$1.8 billion” without always stating NZD, “$5.5B” not labeled NZD, and some year ranges like “2018–2020” without exact years/values), so the requirement is not consistently met across all data.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis into distinct sections addressing each requested dimension (market size, companies, tariffs, projects, risks)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is clearly structured into labeled sections “(a)” through “(e)”, covering market size/growth, companies, tariffs, projects, and risks/opportunities as distinct dimensions.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional market research tone without promotional language, speculation, or unsupported forward claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a formal, analytical market-research style throughout and largely avoids overt promotional phrasing. However, it includes speculative/forward-looking assertions such as “market recovery… forecast to reach $65.4 billion by 2030,” “demand outlook is robust,” and “will be well-positioned,” some of which are not directly supported with the requested 2018–2024 Stats NZ/MBIE evidence.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents historical employment, revenue, or capacity data (2018-2024) in tables or structured year-by-year format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes structured tables with period/year entries for revenue/growth metrics and a separate capacity/spare-capacity/throughput table covering years within 2018–2024 (e.g., 2021–2024). It also provides employment figures in a structured, time-referenced format (2025–2026) alongside discussion of 2018–2024 trends.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses colloquial phrasing, rhetorical questions, or informal register inappropriate for investment-grade research
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, technical tone throughout, using structured headings, tables, and analytical language. It does not use rhetorical questions or casual/colloquial phrasing (e.g., slang, conversational asides) that would be inappropriate for investment-grade research.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses excessive wordiness, filler phrases, or unnecessary elaboration that extends length without adding substantive information
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is extremely long and includes extensive background sections (e.g., multi-page executive summary, lengthy reconciliations, broad global tariff digressions, and many extra project lists) that go well beyond the 2018–2024 NZ structural fabrication scope. It repeatedly adds narrative commentary and expanded context without directly advancing the requested 2018–2024 Stats NZ/MBIE metrics and top-5 fabricator ranking requirements.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Business Demography or equivalent Stats NZ publication for employment figures
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not cite Stats NZ Business Demography (or an equivalent Stats NZ business demography publication) for employment figures. Employment is attributed mainly to IBISWorld and other sources, and the only Stats NZ-related link is a Figure.NZ chart about businesses, not employment from Business Demography.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or equivalent Stats NZ economic survey for revenue/sales data
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not cite Stats NZ’s Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or any equivalent Stats NZ economic survey as the source for revenue/sales data. It relies on IBISWorld for market revenue and uses Stats NZ only for labour market and Figure.NZ business counts, not AES revenue figures.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NZ Customs Working Tariff Document or official NZ tariff schedule for HS code duty rates
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites the NZ Customs “Working Tariff Document” (source [33]) and uses it as the basis for listing HS 7207–7216 duty rates, explicitly referencing NZ Customs tariff schedules.

citation-quality.requirement: References Steel Construction NZ (SCNZ) or industry body for fabrication capacity/throughput data
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references Steel Construction NZ (SCNZ) multiple times for fabrication throughput/capacity data, citing SCNZ “Structural Steel Industry Update” reports (e.g., November 2020, March 2023, September 2023) and using them to support tonnage processed and capacity figures.

citation-quality.requirement: Either references NZ Companies Office as a data source OR notes that private fabricators have limited public financials
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly notes under “Data Limitations and Methodological Notes” that “Pure fabricators … are privately held” and that their revenue figures are only estimates from third-party sources, implying limited public financials for private fabricators.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites specific data sources for at least 2 major infrastructure projects (CRL, Te Kaha, or hospital projects)
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites specific sources for CRL (e.g., City Rail Link official site and executive summary, Auckland Transport update, 1News dated 3 May 2026) and for a hospital project (New Dunedin Hospital) using Health NZ and Beehive.govt.nz links, among others.
